data.title: Restaurant reset: Sweetgreen sees its largest slide ever after earnings and guidance disappoint
data.description:
Sweetgreen (NYSE:SG) traded below $10 on Friday for the first time in more than 20 months. Investors bailed on the restaurant stock after a disappointing Q2 earnings report and sharp cut in guidance.

For the full year, Sweetgreen (NYSE:SG) sees same-store sales growth of -4% to -6%, compared to a prior forecast for 0% and the consensus estimate of -1.5%. Sweetgreen (SG) guided for full-year revenue of $700 million to $715 million vs. a prior outlook for $740 million to $760 million and the consensus estimate of $741 million. Adjusted EBITDA of $10 million to $15 million is anticipated vs. $30 million prior outlook and $25.8 million consensus.

"Sweetgreen's second quarter results reflected a convergence of several headwinds, including macroeconomic pressures, a challenging comparison to last year's strong Q2, and the transition of our loyalty program," highlighted CEO Jonathan Neman. "We're confident in our ability to improve in the back half of 2025. Early signs from our new loyalty program are encouraging, our summer menu is bringing customers back more often, and we remain fully committed to raising the bar on execution across every restaurant."

UBS lowered its 12-month price target to $13 from $19 after the report. The new PT is based on a ~23X multiple of the lowered 2028 EBITDA estimate discounted back. "Despite near-term headwinds, we continue to believe SG's long-term growth levers remain intact, with leading store development, kitchen automation, menu innovation, and loyalty supporting sss and EBITDA growth over the coming years," updated analyst Dennis Geiger.

TD Cowen clipped its price target on Sweetgreen (SG) to $10. "We prefer to model 2025 adjusted EBITDA at the low end of the range, and expect 2026 will trail 15-20% net restaurant growth," advised analyst Andrew Charles.

Oppenheimer moved its 2026 EBITDA forecast to $29 million from $57 million to provide a much-needed reset to its model, and lowered its price target to $18 from $29.

Shares of Sweetgreen (SG) were down 26.7% to $9.29 at 10:21 a.m. after swapping hands as low as $8.81. The intra-day drop was the largest ever for Sweetgreen (SG).

data.title: Veritone targets 32% Q3 revenue growth as VDR pipeline surpasses $20M
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Veritone, Inc. (VERI) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Ryan Scott Steelberg, President and CEO, highlighted that "our revenue of over $24 million for the quarter came in at the high end of our updated guidance in June and is a testament to the demand for our aiWARE platform and our market-leading AI applications and solutions." He stated that organic non-Veritone Hire software revenue grew over 45% in the quarter, with expectations for this growth rate to continue. Steelberg emphasized momentum in Veritone's fastest growth areas, including Veritone Data Refinery (VDR) and the public sector, which now represent the company's largest pipelines and addressable markets.
  • Steelberg reported that Veritone secured 104 new software customers and grew the VDR pipeline by over 100% from Q1. The near-term VDR pipeline now exceeds $20 million. He also announced a sole source contract with the U.S. Air Force, contributing revenue in 2025 and expected to ramp significantly in 2026 and beyond. The public sector pipeline grew to $189 million at quarter-end from $110 million in Q1.
  • Steelberg described cost-saving initiatives expected to generate $10 million in annualized savings and a $10 million equity offering completed in June, stating these moves "have strengthened our financial position and enhanced our ability to execute our strategy and focus on driving growth."
  • Michael L. Zemetra, CFO, stated: "Revenue came in at the top end of our recent guidance with our software products and services, excluding Veritone Hire, growing over 45% year-over-year driven by strong performances across our public sector and commercial enterprise." Zemetra added, "ARR of $62.6 million, up 7% from Q1 2025 of $58.7 million."

Outlook

  • Zemetra provided guidance that Q3 2025 revenue is expected to be between $28 million and $30 million, compared to $22 million from Q3 2024, which represents a 32% increase at the midpoint and a 21% sequential increase from Q2 2025. Software products and services are anticipated to increase over 45% year-over-year, led by public sector and commercial enterprise growth. Public sector revenue is expected to grow over 50% year-over-year, while commercial revenue led by VDR is also projected to grow over 45%.
  • Fiscal 2025 revenue guidance was updated to between $108 million to $115 million, representing a 20% increase at the midpoint year-over-year. Non-GAAP net loss is expected to be between $30 million to $25 million, representing a 33% improvement year-over-year at the midpoint.
  • Management reaffirmed a 100% to 150% public sector revenue growth target for fiscal year 2025 and noted a sales pipeline in excess of $180 million for near-term opportunities in the public sector.

Financial Results

  • Q2 revenue was slightly over $24 million, flat from Q2 2024, with a $1.8 million increase in software products and services offset by a $1.9 million decline in managed services. Public sector revenue grew over 90% year-over-year, and commercial enterprise software products and services increased $0.8 million year-over-year.
  • Gross profit for Q2 GAAP was $15.3 million, with gross margins of 63.9%. Non-GAAP gross margins were 68.9%. VDR gross margins were approximately 40%. Operating loss for Q2 was $19.3 million, an improvement of $1 million year-over-year. Net loss from continuing operations was $26.8 million, an increase year-over-year, primarily due to a change in the estimated fair value of earnout from the divestiture of Veritone One. Non-GAAP net loss from continuing operations improved to $8.7 million from $11.1 million in Q1 2025.
  • Cash and restricted cash were $13.9 million as of June 30, 2025. The company completed a $10 million equity offering in June and reduced consolidated debt to approximately $128 million.

Q&A

  • Jesse Sobelson, D. Boral Capital: Questioned what needs to convert to support the acceleration in growth and about visibility into the guidance. Steelberg responded, "our delta of go get to hit that guide midpoint and hopefully the high end is the smallest it's ever been...the contracted opportunities and the businesses and opportunities that we're currently servicing will support the balance of that opportunity for revenue."
  • Sobelson asked about Veritone's differentiation in regulated industries versus major AI platforms. Steelberg emphasized aiWARE's platform-agnostic, end-to-end stack, and the scale of their audio and video tokenization: "our differentiation is not just understanding and making a very early bet that there were going to be hundreds, thousands, millions of AI models, but we made a bet that...you need to have the orchestration of not just the models but the true understanding of the end-to-end platform that can manage the unstructured and structured data at the same time."
  • Glenn George Mattson, Ladenburg: Asked if more large deals are needed to meet guidance and about the impact of the Air Force contract. Steelberg confirmed that "the OSI Air Force contract alone presents a great, huge opportunity for years to come" and the pipeline is expanding. Mattson also asked about VDR pipeline quantification, with Steelberg explaining the $20 million figure covers high-visibility opportunities over the next 3 to 12 months.
  • Seth Gilbert, UBS: Asked about changes in non-GAAP net loss guidance and public sector revenue growth requirements for Q4. Zemetra explained the narrowing of net loss guidance was due to "the velocity of VDR and the compression on margins." He confirmed a large Q4 uptick is not dependent on a single contract but the Air Force contract is a key driver.
  • Scott Buck, H.C. Wainwright: Asked about what is "in hand" for Q3 guidance and VDR customer concentration. Steelberg said "contracted opportunity provides us probably the best resolution for us to...perform against the guide with the smallest delta of go get that we've had in a very long time," and noted VDR customers are concentrated among the top 50 major players, with increasing engagement.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was positive, with questions focused on execution, visibility into guidance, differentiation, and details on large contracts and pipeline. Sobelson described the quarter as "a great quarter doing exactly what you guys said you were going to do."
  • Management maintained a confident and slightly bullish tone throughout both prepared remarks and Q&A, frequently referencing contract momentum and pipeline visibility. Steelberg said, "we are very, very excited...they're all kind of hitting on all cylinders right now."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone shifted from cautious optimism (due to deal timing slippage in Q1) to high confidence with visibility into revenue and contract execution in Q2. Analysts’ sentiment also became more optimistic, focusing more on growth realization than deal risk.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Management increased guidance, expecting higher Q3 revenue growth (32% at the midpoint vs. previous quarter’s sequential outlook) and updated fiscal 2025 revenue targets from a midpoint of $109.5 million to $111.5 million.
  • The VDR pipeline doubled from early Q1 to Q2, and public sector pipeline grew from $110 million to $189 million.
  • The tone in Q2 was more confident, with management citing a reduced "go get" gap to achieve guidance and increased contract execution.
  • While the previous quarter focused on timing of deal closures, this quarter highlighted execution, contract ramp, and visible contribution from large public sector and commercial contracts.
  • Analysts’ questions shifted from concerns over deal timing and macro headwinds to growth realization and contract scaling.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited continued softness in managed services and delays in certain public sector deals as challenges, with cost-saving initiatives and structural improvements underway.
  • Margin compression from VDR as it scales was noted by Zemetra, who said, "while we raised the top end of the revenue guide, we tightened on the lower end or...the higher end on the non-GAAP net loss as a result of that margin compression."
  • Concentration risk in VDR among a relatively small group of large tech and AI companies was discussed as a structural feature of the market.

Final Takeaway

Veritone management emphasized that the company is experiencing a period of accelerated growth driven by strong performance in both its VDR and public sector segments, underpinned by substantial pipeline expansion, a major U.S. Air Force contract, and increased visibility into future revenue. The company’s cost-saving actions and capital raise were highlighted as strengthening its financial position, while management expressed high confidence in achieving updated guidance and realizing further growth through 2025, supported by ongoing contract execution and broadening customer engagement in high-demand AI markets.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Fubo signals Q4 2025 or Q1 2026 close for Hulu+ Live TV merger while launching new sports initiatives
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: fuboTV Inc. (FUBO) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO David Gandler opened the call highlighting Fubo's first quarter of positive adjusted EBITDA, calling it "an important milestone for our business." Gandler reported that "our global streaming business exceeded both revenue and subscriber expectations in the second quarter." In North America, total revenue was $371 million with 1,356,000 paid subscribers, while Rest of World revenue was $8.7 million with 349,000 paid subscribers.
  • Gandler stated the company has "filed a preliminary proxy statement seeking shareholder approval of our agreement with the Walt Disney Company to combine Fubo with Hulu+ Live TV," and maintained the anticipated timeline to close the transaction in "the fourth quarter of calendar year 2025 or the first quarter of calendar year 2026."
  • The company announced the upcoming launch of "Fubo Sports, a skinny content service for sports fans," and noted the recent launch of a pay-per-view feature, allowing both subscribers and nonsubscribers to access live events on a one-off basis. Gandler referenced the content partnership with DAZN, saying this expands Fubo's reach and gives subscribers access to the DAZN1 channel.
  • CFO John Janedis stated, "We delivered North America revenue of $371 million and North America paid subscribers of 1.36 million, and we are pleased with our ability to deliver outperformance in the second quarter versus expectations." Janedis also noted, "Ad revenue in North America totaled $25.5 million, a 2% year-over-year decline, primarily due to the loss of certain ad-insertable content from Warner Bros. Discovery and TelevisaUnivision."
  • Janedis emphasized, "Net loss narrowed to $8 million or $0.02 per share compared to a loss of $25.8 million or $0.08 per share a year ago," and highlighted second quarter adjusted EBITDA of $20.7 million.

Outlook

  • No explicit financial guidance figures for future quarters were provided in the transcript, but management reiterated its "ongoing focus on driving operating leverage in the model to best position the company for long-term growth."
  • Gandler reaffirmed excitement about the Hulu+ Live TV combination, stating, "The anticipated time line to close this transaction is currently the fourth quarter of calendar year 2025 or the first quarter of calendar year 2026."
  • Management discussed the expectation of a "typical seasonal uptick alongside of reactivations" in the fall sports season.

Financial Results

  • Second quarter North America revenue was reported as $371 million, with paid subscribers of 1.36 million.
  • Rest of World revenue was $8.7 million, with 349,000 subscribers.
  • North America ad revenue was $25.5 million, a 2% year-over-year decline, attributed to the loss of certain ad-insertable content.
  • Net loss was $8 million or $0.02 per share, compared to a loss of $25.8 million or $0.08 per share a year ago.
  • Adjusted EBITDA reached $20.7 million, marking the company's first-ever positive adjusted EBITDA quarter.
  • Net cash used in operating activities was $34.6 million; free cash flow was negative $37.7 million. The company ended the quarter with over $285 million in cash, cash equivalents, and restricted cash.

Q&A

  • Patrick William Sholl, Barrington Research, asked about subscriber expectations for Q3 and marketing in a competitive landscape. John Janedis responded that July "finished in line...with what we expected in terms of subscribers," and that "with the fall sports season starting soon, we would expect to see the typical seasonal uptick alongside of reactivations."
  • Laura Anne Martin, Needham, questioned the impact and strategy behind the French acquisition and free streaming services. Gandler explained the integration of technology teams and ongoing negotiations for sports rights in France, expressing optimism for upcoming opportunities.
  • Martin also asked about competitive positioning and ideal consumer experiences. Gandler noted, "We've seen a nice uptick on the stand-alone offers that we put out there," and emphasized the need for "broad packages" and value at different price points.
  • Alicia Spring Reese, Wedbush, inquired about ad trends and the impact of tariffs. Janedis cited continued softness in auto advertising but noted growth in retail e-commerce and tech. He described FAST channels as "a modest positive tailwind, if you will, to the overall ad growth."
  • Joseph Louis Spiezio, BTIG, asked about the sustainability of EBITDA profitability. Janedis said, "Historically speaking, 2Q has been our strongest adjusted EBITDA quarter of the year," reflecting seasonality in profitability.
  • Douglas Middleton Arthur, Huber Research, asked about subscriber guidance and the effect of content partner terminations. Janedis pointed to better-than-expected retention and conversion on the Latino product after the Univision drop, and Gandler described stabilization efforts in advertising and focus on skinny sports services.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on sustainability of profitability, competitive positioning, subscriber retention, and advertising trends; tone was generally constructive but probing, particularly regarding future profitability and product strategy.
  • Management expressed confidence, frequently referencing milestones and emphasizing operational discipline. Janedis described being "extremely pleased" and Gandler consistently projected optimism about strategic direction and upcoming initiatives.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone shifted from cautious optimism focused on path to profitability to celebratory confidence about achieving positive adjusted EBITDA. Analyst tone remained consistent, with a continued focus on core metrics and emerging challenges.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter marked Fubo's first positive adjusted EBITDA, while the previous quarter reported negative adjusted EBITDA of $1.4 million.
  • North America revenue declined from $407.9 million in Q1 to $371 million in Q2, and subscribers decreased from 1.47 million to 1.36 million. Rest of World revenue increased, while subscribers continued to decline.
  • Management in Q2 highlighted new product initiatives (Fubo Sports, pay-per-view) and the DAZN partnership, compared to Q1's focus on launching skinny bundles and preparing for the Hulu+ Live TV merger.
  • Guidance language in Q2 was less explicit, with no new financial targets provided, but the company maintained momentum toward the Hulu+ Live TV merger timeline.
  • Analysts continued to press on profitability and retention, but management's confidence was more pronounced following the adjusted EBITDA milestone.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged the "loss of certain ad-insertable content from Warner Bros. Discovery and TelevisaUnivision" as a headwind for advertising revenue.
  • The competitive streaming environment and consumer price sensitivity remain ongoing challenges.
  • Subscriber attrition, particularly in the Rest of World segment, was noted, but management described ongoing negotiations for new sports rights and technology deployment as mitigation strategies.
  • Analysts questioned the sustainability of EBITDA profitability, the impact of content partner terminations, and the risk of further audience or revenue declines without new content deals.

Final Takeaway

Fubo achieved a milestone by delivering its first quarter of positive adjusted EBITDA, driven by disciplined execution and focused product innovation. The company remains on track with its pending merger with Hulu+ Live TV, targeting a close in late 2025 or early 2026. Despite ongoing industry challenges, Fubo is advancing new sports-centric offerings and expanding its content partnerships, while maintaining financial flexibility and operational momentum.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Biggest stock movers Friday: TWLO, TTWO, TTD, and more
data.description:
Stock futures rose on Friday as investors responded to political and monetary policy developments, led by President Donald Trump’s nomination of Stephen Miran to the Federal Reserve’s Board of Governors, a move seen as pushing the Fed toward a more aggressive interest rate-cutting stance that could boost equities and weigh on the U.S. dollar.

Here are some of Friday's biggest stock movers:

Biggest stock gainers

  • Take-Two Interactive Software (NASDAQ:TTWO) +4% – Shares rose after the company beat on key metrics in its FQ1 earnings report, raised its annual bookings forecast, and reaffirmed the launch schedule for Grand Theft Auto VI. For FY2026, the GTA and NBA2K publisher increased its net bookings guidance to $6.05B-$6.15B (midpoint $6.10B) from $5.90B-$6B, topping the $6.04B consensus. Adjusted EBITDA guidance was raised to $827M-$886M (midpoint $856.5M) from $793M-$847M, above the $833.6M estimate. CEO Strauss Zelnick said the results reflect ongoing demand for core franchises and the increasingly diversified and successful nature of its business.
  • Skillz (NYSE:SKLZ) +6% - Shares climbed after the company's Q2 results topped estimates, with revenue up 8% Y/Y and paying monthly active users jumping 18% sequentially and 20% from last year to the highest since Q3 2023. The company highlighted progress in player engagement and retention, the rollout of its $75M Developer Accelerator program, and disciplined expense management that drove Y/Y improvements in adjusted EBITDA loss. Skillz ended the quarter with over $238M in cash and restricted cash, providing flexibility to invest in growth initiatives.
  • Block (NYSE:XYZ) +8% - Shares jumped after the company raised its full-year gross profit guidance and issued a strong Q3 outlook. For Q2, gross payment volume and gross profit exceeded Wall Street expectations, though revenue and adjusted EPS missed. The parent of Square and Cash App expects a Q3 gross profit of $2.60B, above the $2.54B consensus, and an adjusted operating income of $460M vs. the $425M estimate. Block now sees a 2025 gross profit of $10.2B, above the $9.94B consensus and prior guidance of $9.96B.
  • Gilead Sciences (GILD) +8% – Shares rose after the California-based biotech posted better-than-expected Q2 results and raised its full-year sales and profit outlook, driven by strong demand for its HIV products. The company lifted its adjusted profit forecast to $7.95–$8.25 per share from $7.70–$8.10, compared with analyst consensus of $8, and increased its 2025 net product sales guidance to $28.30B–$28.70B from $28.20B–$28.60B, vs. expectations of $28.73B.
  • SoundHound (SOUN) +24% – Shares surged after Q2 results beat estimates and the company raised its 2025 outlook, prompting largely positive analyst reactions. Wedbush maintained an Outperform rating and lifted its price target to $16 from $15, citing 217% Y/Y revenue growth to $42.7M (vs. $32.9M expected) on new client wins, cross-sell deals, and momentum with its conversational AI platform Amelia. The firm noted the guidance hike to $160M–$178M from $157M–$177M was likely conservative. Piper Sandler reiterated a Neutral with a $12 target, seeing profitability and gross margins ahead of expectations but balanced risk-reward. Cantor also kept a Neutral with an $8.50 target, highlighting organic growth, a China auto deal, solid enterprise subscription close rates, and rising ARPU across its platform.

Biggest stock losers

  • Pinterest (NYSE:PINS) -10% – Shares slumped after the company reported mixed Q2 results, missing profit expectations. Revenue rose 17% Y/Y to $998.2M, topping estimates of $975.5M, while monthly active users hit a record 578M, up 11% from last year. Net income and adjusted EBITDA both grew 33%, with EBITDA reaching $250.8M and margins improving to 25% from 22%. For Q3, the company expects revenue of $1.033B-$1.053B, reflecting 15%-17% growth, above the consensus of $1.03B, and forecasts adjusted EBITDA of $282M-$302M.
  • The Trade Desk (NASDAQ:TTD) -38% - Shares slid after Q2 results, guidance, and a surprise CFO transition disappointed investors. Revenue guidance for Q3 came in at $717M, just above the $716.2M consensus, while adjusted EBITDA is expected at $277M vs. $275.7M forecast. Longtime CFO Laura Schenkein will step down after over a decade with the firm, to be succeeded by Alex Kayyal.
  • Twilio (NYSE:TWLO) -18% – Shares dropped despite beating Q2 estimates, as Q3 EPS guidance of $1.01–$1.06 fell short of the $1.14 consensus. The revenue outlook of $1.25B–$1.26B topped the $1.22B forecast. The company reiterated its FY25 non-GAAP operating income target of $850M–$875M and lifted free cash flow guidance to $875M–$900M from $850M–$875M.
  • Under Armour (UA) (UAA) -18% - Shares plummeted after FQ1 earnings missed estimates, with revenue down 4% Y/Y. North America sales dropped 5%, while international revenue slipped 1%, as EMEA grew 10%, Asia-Pacific fell 10%, and Latin America declined 15%. Wholesale revenue decreased 5%, direct-to-consumer dipped 3%, with e-commerce down 12% but owned stores up 1%. For FQ2, the company expects a 6–7% revenue decline, including a low-double-digit drop in North America, high-single-digit growth in EMEA, and a low-teens decline in Asia-Pacific, with EPS seen at $0.01–$0.02 vs. $0.12 consensus. Management also warned of margin pressure from tariffs and an unfavorable channel mix.

data.title: TruBridge raises adjusted EBITDA guidance to $62M–$67M while lowering revenue outlook for 2025
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: TruBridge, Inc. (TBRG) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Christopher L. Fowler, President and CEO, opened by highlighting steady progress toward long-term goals, particularly in bookings, profitability, and cash flow, and announced signing the CMS interoperability framework pledge. Fowler stated, "By reinforcing our focus on interoperability and modern data infrastructure, we're working to make real-time secure patient data accessible anytime and anywhere it's needed."
  • Fowler detailed ongoing transformation initiatives, including a partnership with a third-party consultant to improve operations, drive efficiencies, and unlock customer value. He reported bookings of $25.6 million on a TCV basis and revenue of $85.7 million, noting that revenue was at the lower end of expectations but adjusted EBITDA of $13.7 million came in slightly ahead of midpoint.
  • Fowler announced a revised guidance, "We're lowering the top end of our revenue range and... raising our adjusted EBITDA range as well." He attributed the revenue guidance revision to CBO client retention and delayed revenue recognition from larger bookings.
  • Strategic actions included a temporary pause in global hiring, establishment of a physical office in India targeted for 2026, and key leadership hires focused on offshore service levels. Fowler said, "We have significantly enhanced our resource management efforts for CBO... instituted a temporary pause in Q2 in our global hiring efforts to rightsizing staffing needs while continuing to invest in our people through training programs."
  • Fowler introduced a collaboration with Microsoft to integrate Microsoft Dragon Copilot into TruBridge's EHR solution, aiming to bring advanced AI-driven speech recognition and efficiency to rural and community healthcare clients.
  • Vinay Bassi, CFO, stated, "For the first half of 2025, we generated $14.5 million in cash flow from operations, an increase of $2.8 million compared to the first 6 months of the prior year. Year-to-date, free cash flow... was $5.5 million, up $3.4 million compared to the first 6 months of 2024."
  • Bassi highlighted improvement in accounts receivable and DSO, leverage ratio improvement to 2.4x, and continued focus on cost efficiencies, with gross margins in Q2 at 52%. He added, "The midpoint of our revised guidance reflects an adjusted EBITDA margin of approximately 18.5%, which is up 200 basis points compared to the previous year, and we remain confident that we can end the year around 20%."

Outlook

  • TruBridge revised its full-year revenue guidance to $345 million to $350 million, lowering the top end from the previous range of $345 million to $360 million.
  • The company raised its adjusted EBITDA guidance to a range of $62 million to $67 million, up from a previous range of $60 million to $66 million.
  • For Q3, revenue is expected to be between $85 million and $87 million, and adjusted EBITDA between $14 million and $16 million.
  • Fowler explained, "With respect to this group of clients, we expect renewals to remain in this range in the back half of 2025, but with significant improvement in 2026 based on our performance."

Financial Results

  • Bookings reached $25.6 million on a TCV basis, up from $22 million sequentially and $23 million year-over-year. On an ACV basis, bookings were $19.6 million, up 13% sequentially.
  • Q2 revenue was $85.7 million. Financial Health revenue was $54.3 million, and Patient Care revenue was $31.4 million.
  • Gross margins increased to 52%. Financial Health gross margins were 46%, and Patient Care gross margins were 62%, each improving over the prior year.
  • Operating expenses were $40.8 million, representing 48% of revenue, compared to 52% in Q2 last year.
  • Cash and cash equivalents ended Q2 at $12.3 million.

Q&A

  • Sarah Elizabeth James, Cantor Fitzgerald: Asked for more detail on scaling and timing of savings initiatives. Vinay Bassi responded, "Last year, we did cost efficiencies... with the team settling in and getting now the benefit of our third-party consultant, we are now cracking a little more complex part of the puzzles... most likely from the quarter 4 onwards, start realizing some savings."
  • James inquired about the impact of the OB3 bill on client planning and contract pace. Fowler replied, "I do suspect that it could have some potential headwind to the second half of the year. We haven't seen that manifest just yet, but you never know exactly how that's going to play out."
  • Jeffrey Robert Garro, Stephens: Asked about demand and ability to maintain bookings above $20 million. Fowler stated, "We continue to feel good about our performance on the bookings front... we're seeing some nice trends in all the area."
  • Garro requested more color on retention trends. Fowler explained, "On the Financial Health, obviously, the story is the journey that we're on right now of making sure that we've got our offshore, our global operation buttoned up so that the delivery to our customers is what it always has been."
  • Eugene Mark Mannheimer, Freedom Capital Markets: Asked about the third-party consultant and client attrition. Fowler said, "The short answer to question one is no. We will not. And getting into the attrition side, I would say it's a mixed bag, but the vast majority are going back in-house."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts displayed a neutral-to-cautious tone, probing the scalability of cost initiatives, the potential headwind from policy changes, and the sustainability of bookings and retention. There was notable interest in operational improvements and external impacts on client behavior.
  • Management maintained a confident and transparent tone, emphasizing progress, adaptability, and optimism regarding medium-term operational goals. Fowler used phrases such as "we remain confident" and "I feel confident that we will begin to make headway," showing a steady assurance despite acknowledging challenges.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s sentiment remained confident but more focused on addressing client retention and timing challenges, while analysts exhibited increased caution concerning external headwinds and operational execution.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for full-year adjusted EBITDA was raised, while revenue guidance was trimmed at the top end; in Q1, guidance was maintained for revenue and increased for EBITDA, whereas Q2 saw a more conservative revenue outlook.
  • Management continued to emphasize cost optimization, offshoring, and standardization initiatives, with a heavier focus this quarter on client retention and operational execution, particularly in the Financial Health segment.
  • Analysts in both quarters focused on the sustainability of cost savings, scalability of initiatives, and external policy impacts, but Q2 questions reflected heightened concern about client retention and policy-related headwinds.
  • Key metrics such as cash flow, margins, and bookings showed sequential and year-over-year improvement, though revenue growth moderated.
  • Management’s tone was consistently confident, but discussions in Q2 included greater emphasis on addressing operational setbacks and timeline delays.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited CBO client retention and delayed revenue recognition from larger deals as factors impacting the revenue outlook.
  • Analysts raised concerns about potential headwinds from policy changes, specifically the OB3 bill, and their effect on client planning and sales cycles.
  • The majority of client attrition is attributed to clients moving in-house, with some skepticism about the offshore model among smaller hospitals.
  • Fowler acknowledged the multi-quarter journey to operationalize retention improvements and standardize global operations.

Final Takeaway

TruBridge’s Q2 2025 call highlighted continual progress in bookings, margin expansion, and operational initiatives, supported by a strategic overhaul of resource management and leadership additions. While top-line revenue guidance was trimmed due to client retention and delayed recognition from larger deals, management increased adjusted EBITDA expectations and signaled confidence in long-term improvements, underpinned by offshoring, cost optimization, and technology innovation partnerships. The company remains focused on stabilizing client retention, executing on operational efficiencies, and leveraging its collaboration with Microsoft to drive future growth and improved client outcomes.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Capricor rises as FDA meeting renews hopes for rejected cell therapy
data.description:
• Capricor Therapeutics (NASDAQ:CAPR) added ~9% on Friday after the company announced a meeting with the U.S. FDA to discuss the path forward for its recently rejected lead cell therapy candidate, Deramiocel.
  • In July, shares of the San Diego, California-based biotech dropped after the FDA issued a complete response letter rejecting its Biologics License Application for Deramiocel, targeted at cardiomyopathy in patients with Duchenne muscular dystrophy, a rare muscular disorder.
  • The Type A meeting with the FDA is focused on discussing “the regulatory path” for the BLA, the company announced in a press release in the premarket on Friday.
  • To accommodate the meeting, Capricor (NASDAQ:CAPR) has also rescheduled the release of its Q2 2025 financials to Aug. 11 in the post-market after initially planning to announce the results on Aug. 13.

data.title: Brighthouse Financial outlines completion of separate hedging strategies for VA and Shield by end of September while advancing capital-focused initiatives
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Brighthouse Financial (BHF) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Eric Thomas Steigerwalt, President and CEO, highlighted that "Brighthouse Financial continued to make progress against its capital-focused strategic initiatives. As a reminder, those initiatives are designed to improve capital efficiency, unlock capital and remain within our target combined risk-based capital or RBC ratio range in normal market conditions." Steigerwalt noted strong sales results, additional deposits through BlackRock's LifePath Paycheck, disciplined expense management, and a solid capital and liquidity position with liquid assets at the holding company in excess of $900 million as of June 30. He emphasized, "In the second quarter, our estimated combined RBC ratio was between 405% and 425%, within our target range in normal market conditions."
  • Shield sales totaled $1.9 billion in the quarter, with year-to-date Shield sales at $3.9 billion. Fixed annuities contributed $500 million to sales. Life insurance sales reached $33 million for the quarter and $69 million year-to-date, which Steigerwalt described as a record, noting a "21% increase compared with the same period in 2024." The company received $176 million in deposits through BlackRock's LifePath Paycheck product.
  • Second quarter corporate expenses were $202 million on a pretax basis, "down from $239 million in the first quarter, and up slightly from $200 million in the second quarter of 2024." Brighthouse returned $43 million to shareholders through common stock repurchases in the quarter.
  • Edward Allen Spehar, CFO, stated, "As of June 30, the estimated combined risk-based capital or RBC ratio was between 405% and 425%, within our target range of 400% to 450% in normal market conditions. The statutory combined total adjusted capital, or TAC, was approximately $5.6 billion at June 30 compared with approximately $5.5 billion at March 31."
  • Spehar reiterated, "We made some modifications to our hedges at the beginning of the third quarter, and we plan to complete the transition to our revised strategy, managing the VA and Shield businesses separately by the end of September."

Outlook

  • Management indicated that its capital-focused initiatives remain ongoing, with the completion of the separate hedging strategy for in-force variable annuity and first-generation Shield blocks targeted for the end of September. There were no explicit updates to EPS or revenue guidance in the transcript. Steigerwalt reiterated that "Our involvement with [BlackRock's LifePath Paycheck] product to enable Brighthouse to reach new customers through the worksite channel, and we remain extremely excited about its success to date." Expense discipline remains a focus, with expectations for continued well-controlled expense management.

Financial Results

  • Adjusted earnings for the quarter were $198 million or $3.43 per share, compared with $245 million in the first quarter and $346 million in the second quarter of 2024. Spehar explained, "The second quarter adjusted earnings of $198 million were approximately $60 million below our quarterly average run rate expectations, driven by lower alternative investment income and a lower underwriting margin."
  • The alternative investment portfolio yield was 1.5%, with income $32 million below the quarterly average run rate expectation. Annuities segment adjusted earnings were $332 million, while the Life segment reported an adjusted loss of $26 million. The Run-off segment had an adjusted loss of $83 million, and Corporate and Other segment reported an adjusted loss of $25 million.
  • Total annuity sales reached $2.6 billion, up 16% sequentially and 8% year-over-year. Shield sales were highlighted as a major contributor.

Q&A

  • Thomas George Gallagher, Evercore ISI, asked about actuarial review timing and potential statutory charges. Spehar responded, "We're in the process of the VA, Shield separation work here, which is going to have an impact on our ending third quarter balance sheet relative to the ending second quarter balance sheet...we will the stat review again this time on an annuity basis in the fourth quarter and the GAAP review in the third quarter."
  • Gallagher questioned the impact of hedging strategy separation on capital generation. Spehar replied that the separation "will introduce simplification, more transparency and allow for more effective management of the block of business. I also think, overall, we're going to see less volatility in our results over time."
  • Jamminder Singh Bhullar, JPMorgan, asked about buybacks and stand-alone viability. Steigerwalt stated, "We have been a returner of capital," and on strategy, "our journey has brought us to the place where it is obvious we are a premier carrier in this industry."
  • Suneet Kamath, Jefferies, raised questions on unassigned surplus, statutory loss, and competitive positioning. Spehar explained unassigned funds are "a technical consideration, not a fundamental one" and that dividends to the holding company remain in the three-year financial plan. On Bermuda captives and alternative asset management, Steigerwalt said, "we're thinking about that every single day."
  • Wilma Carter Jackson Burdis, Raymond James, inquired about capital movement and sales appetite. Spehar clarified that the TAC increase was not due to hedging actions and that management remains committed to maintaining sales momentum across product lines.
  • Additional questions addressed capital in the Delaware Reinsurance Company, preferred stock dividends, implications of hedging changes, and future cash flow projections.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was predominantly neutral to slightly negative, with recurring questions around capital adequacy, hedging strategy changes, statutory losses, and capital return. Analysts probed for clarity on technical statutory items, potential charges, and the impact of strategic transitions, often pressing for more quantification or future outlook.
  • Management maintained a steady, sometimes defensive tone, especially when addressing technical capital questions and market rumors. Phrases such as "we don't comment on rumors and speculation" and "it's too early to talk about -- to quantify what the impact of separation will be" were used. Confidence was evident in remarks about the underlying business and strategic progress, with Spehar asserting, "the foundation of our financial and risk management strategy is unwavering."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both management and analysts sustained a focus on capital and balance sheet strength, but the Q2 dialogue showed more urgency around the hedging transition and its implications.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language remained focused on capital discipline and ongoing strategic initiatives, with a notable escalation in the timeline for completing the separation of hedging strategies by the end of September.
  • Strategic focus sharpened around the simplification of hedge management and transparency, compared to broader capital efficiency messaging in Q1.
  • Analysts continued to focus on statutory results, cash flows, and capital return, but Q2 brought intensified scrutiny on technical statutory metrics and the impact of hedging revisions.
  • Key metrics shifted: RBC ratio moved from 420%-440% in Q1 to 405%-425% in Q2, and adjusted earnings declined from $245 million to $198 million.
  • Management tone remained confident in strategic direction, yet more frequently referenced uncertainty regarding the ultimate impact of pending transitions.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited the need to manage complexity in legacy liabilities and the importance of executing the hedging strategy transition as a key challenge.
  • Adverse mortality and lower alternative investment income were identified as drivers of lower earnings.
  • Analysts raised concerns about statutory losses, unassigned surplus, future capital generation, and the technical intricacies of the statutory capital framework.
  • Management stated ongoing dialogue with regulators regarding extraordinary dividends and highlighted continued expense discipline as a mitigation strategy.

Final Takeaway

Brighthouse Financial's second quarter 2025 call emphasized ongoing execution of capital-focused initiatives, highlighted by the separate management of variable annuity and Shield hedging strategies targeted for completion by the end of September. The company reported robust sales in annuities and life insurance, maintained its capital ratios within target, and reiterated its commitment to expense discipline and shareholder capital return, while navigating technical challenges and market scrutiny on its statutory results and future capital generation.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Chesapeake Utilities raises 2025 capital expenditure guidance to $425M while reaffirming EPS outlook
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Chesapeake Utilities Corporation (CPK) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Jeffry M. Householder, President, CEO & Chairman, highlighted continued capital investment and regulatory achievements, stating "Our growth trajectory continues as we've expanded our capital investment program, achieved regulatory success and maintained a strong balance sheet while financing future growth." He reported adjusted earnings per share of $1.04 for Q2 2025, up 21% from the prior year, noting this marks the fourth consecutive quarterly increase following the Florida City Gas acquisition. The company reaffirmed full year 2025 EPS guidance of $6.15 to $6.35 per share and increased its 2025 capital expenditure guidance to a range of $375 million to $425 million, a $50 million increase over the prior range.
  • Householder described above-average customer growth in both Delmarva and Florida, with year-to-date residential customer growth of 4.2% and 3% respectively, and detailed the launch of six major capital projects in the first half, including the Duncan Plains data center project in Ohio.
  • James F. Moriarty, Executive VP, General Counsel, provided regulatory updates, confirming final orders for rate cases in Maryland, Delaware, and Florida, totaling $18.2 million in annual revenue increases. He indicated a final order on the Florida City Gas depreciation study is expected in Q4 2025.
  • Beth W. Cooper, Executive VP, CFO, Treasurer & Assistant Corporate Secretary, stated, "Adjusted gross margin was approximately $143 million, up 13% from the second quarter of 2024... adjusted net income of approximately $24 million, up 26%... and adjusted earnings per share, up $0.18 to $1.04, a 21% increase over the second quarter of 2024." Cooper outlined financial performance drivers and detailed the company reaching its equity capitalization target of 50% and exceeding $3 billion in total book capitalization for the first time.

Outlook

  • Management reaffirmed full year 2025 adjusted EPS guidance of $6.15 to $6.35 per share, with Jeffry M. Householder noting that "this range does assume a successful outcome in 2025 on the Florida City Gas depreciation study." The company raised its 2025 capital expenditure guidance to $375 million to $425 million, citing strong investment pace and project pipeline.

Financial Results

  • Chesapeake Utilities reported adjusted gross margin of approximately $143 million for Q2 2025, a 13% increase year-over-year, and adjusted net income of approximately $24 million, up 26%. Adjusted EPS reached $1.04, up 21% from the prior year period. The Regulated segment contributed approximately $118 million in adjusted gross margin, up 14%, and regulated operating income increased 28% to $52 million. The Unregulated Energy segment grew adjusted gross margin to $25 million, up 7%, with Marlin Gas Services and Full Circle Dairy contributing incremental margin, partially offset by reduced propane margins.
  • The company issued $66.2 million of equity in the first half, achieved its 50% equity capitalization target, and exceeded $3 billion in total book capitalization. New long-term debt was issued at a blended 5.04% coupon, enhancing debt capacity and liquidity.
  • The annualized dividend per share was increased to $2.74, reflecting a 7% growth rate and supporting a long-term dividend CAGR of 9%.

Q&A

  • Michael Christopher Brown, Barclays: Asked whether the successful outcome in the FCG depreciation study was assumed in the 2025 guidance midpoint and about capital funding plans. Beth W. Cooper confirmed, "yes, considered within the full guidance range was a successful outcome on the Florida City Gas depreciation study," and explained that funding will continue to adhere to the target capital structure, with flexibility aligned to project timing.
  • Michael E. Gaugler, Janney Montgomery Scott: Inquired about new project discussions in Pennsylvania and Ohio, and effects of PJM power auction on natural gas demand. Cooper stated the business development group is "always looking at things, but we don't have anything at this point that we could disclose around any other particular projects," while Householder added ongoing pursuit of opportunities like the data center-related project in Ohio. On PJM, Householder said, "we are certainly talking to a number of larger commercial and industrial customers... looking for increasing their natural gas capabilities."
  • Paul Basch Michael Fremont, Ladenburg: Asked about the schedule and expectations for the depreciation case. James F. Moriarty responded, "staff has been supportive of our approach here... we're confident that the filing will be received and be successful."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts showed a neutral tone, seeking clarity on guidance assumptions, funding strategy, and regulatory outcomes, without pressing skepticism or negative signals.
  • Management maintained a confident and composed tone, frequently reiterating guidance, financial strength, and project execution. Cooper emphasized, "we will continue to pursue a capital structure that is around 50% plus equity to total capitalization."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone remained steady and confident, while analysts' tone was similarly neutral and focused on execution details.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The company increased its capital expenditure guidance by $50 million compared to Q1, reflecting higher investment and project momentum. EPS guidance was reaffirmed and remains contingent on the outcome of the Florida City Gas depreciation study, consistent with Q1 language.
  • Customer growth rates in both Delmarva and Florida remained robust. Management continued to highlight operational and regulatory achievements, and financial performance improved with a higher adjusted EPS growth rate in Q2 compared to Q1.
  • Analysts in both quarters focused on regulatory milestones and capital allocation, with their tone remaining neutral and fact-driven.
  • Strategic priorities continue to emphasize capital deployment, regulatory management, and operational transformation.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management flagged that the full year EPS guidance assumes a successful outcome in the Florida City Gas depreciation study, with a commission hearing expected in September and a final order anticipated in Q4.
  • Cost increases in the Worcester Resiliency Upgrade project were managed through FERC-approved rate adjustments, with ongoing construction and margin contributions now expected in 2026.
  • The company continues to monitor regulatory outcomes and the timing of major capital projects as key variables impacting financial results.

Final Takeaway

Chesapeake Utilities delivered another quarter of double-digit earnings and margin growth, underpinned by ongoing capital investment, successful regulatory outcomes, and robust customer expansion in core markets. The company raised its 2025 capital expenditure guidance to $375 million to $425 million and reaffirmed EPS guidance of $6.15 to $6.35 per share, with progress on major projects and a strengthened financial position. Continued execution on capital deployment, regulatory strategy, and operational excellence remain central to sustaining top quartile shareholder returns through 2028.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Victory Capital outlines $500M buyback plan and $110M synergy target while expanding global ETF reach
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Victory Capital Holdings, Inc. (VCTR) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO David Craig Brown highlighted that "total client assets increased by 76% quarter-over-quarter, reaching more than $300 billion, which is a record high for a quarter end." Brown emphasized continued sales momentum, noting "quarterly gross long-term flows accelerating to $15.4 billion, and net outflows coming in at just $660 million," marking the third consecutive quarter of improving long-term flows.
  • Brown announced the completion of the Amundi U.S. acquisition on April 1, stating this "resulted in significantly increasing our size and scale while also substantially enhancing the diversification of our business." He noted integration progress, reporting "$70 million of net expense synergies on a run rate basis," and outlined expectations to realize the remaining $40 million over the next 15 months.
  • Brown detailed the launch of new products, including the VictoryShares Pioneer Asset-Based Income ETF and two new international free cash flow ETFs, bringing ETF assets under management to $15 billion at the end of June, "up nearly 90% from the same time last year."
  • Brown stated, "the Board has authorized an increase to our share repurchase plan from $200 million to $500 million, the largest in our history." He also signaled ongoing acquisition interest: "We are extremely active from an acquisition perspective, evaluating potential opportunities."
  • Michael Dennis Policarpo, President, CFO & Chief Administrative Officer, reported, "Revenue increased to $351.2 million, which was up 60% from the first quarter." He added, "average assets for the second quarter rose to $285 billion which was 64% higher quarter-over-quarter," and clarified that "adjusted EBITDA was $178.5 million, which is 53% higher than the first quarter."

Outlook

  • Management maintained long-term adjusted EBITDA margin guidance at 49%. Brown confirmed, "we would expect the fee rate to be in the range of 46 to 47 basis points" for the third quarter and beyond.
  • Policarpo indicated, "our normal effective tax rate will be approximately 25%, which is unchanged from previous guidance."
  • Brown expressed optimism regarding global expansion: "We are very optimistic about the opportunity to expand business around the globe." He projected the integration of Amundi distribution would enable registration and go-to-market for new products in the next few quarters.
  • The Board declared a regular quarterly cash dividend of $0.49, payable on September 25.

Financial Results

  • Revenue was reported as $351.2 million for the quarter. Adjusted EBITDA was $178.5 million, with an adjusted EBITDA margin of 50.8%.
  • GAAP operating margin was 26.8%, reflecting $53 million in acquisition-related restructuring and integration costs. Adjusted net income with tax benefit was $132.8 million, or $1.57 per diluted share.
  • Average assets for the quarter were $285 billion. The company ended the quarter with $108 million in cash and a net leverage ratio of 1.2x, its lowest since IPO.
  • The company repurchased 439,000 shares and returned a total of $71 million to shareholders during the quarter.

Q&A

  • Randy Binner, B. Riley Securities: Asked about the pace of nonrecurring expense wind-down. Policarpo answered, "$26 million of that is truly deal related and onetime...will not recur. And then...about $14 million of expense really associated with the extraction of the synergies to date...it will start to decline. There's definitely onetime in Q2, and then we'll start to see them decline over the next several quarters."
  • Binner: Inquired about fixed income asset mix. Brown responded, "we love the fixed income asset class in general...we have a really good product set for any environment...from a product perspective, from a distribution perspective, and the performance across the board with our two franchises is excellent."
  • Michael Cho, JPMorgan: Asked about Amundi partnership expectations for non-U.S. distribution. Brown stated, "The strategic partnership with Amundi really has given us the ability to sell our products outside of the U.S. to really all through Europe and also through Asia."
  • Cho: Asked about margin drivers. Brown answered, "we've kept our guidance at 49%. The margins will ebb and flow going forward as we continue to invest in the business."
  • Alexander Blostein, Goldman Sachs: Asked about organic growth aspirations. Brown noted, "This quarter, we were net outflow $660 million...as we globalize our business as we continue to expand our product set, we want to be able to grow the business organically."
  • Blostein: Asked about onetime revenue realization. Policarpo stated, "it's hard to do because there's a bunch of different components that factor into it...not easy to quantify. It's immaterial ultimately, to the nature of what we saw at the 49.4 basis points."
  • Benjamin Budish, Barclays: Sought clarity on onetime realization fee nature and margin impact. Policarpo explained, "it's really about how we are able to recognize certain revenue and revenue realization associated with certain products that we have...there really wasn't a significant impact to our overall margins as a result because of how the expenses are offset to any change in revenue."
  • Budish: Asked about current quarter flow momentum. Brown said, "we are still integrating our sales teams...I'd say we're ahead of where I thought we would be, but we've got a lot of work to do."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was generally positive, with questions focusing on integration progress, expense normalization, fixed income growth, and international expansion. Several analysts probed for more clarity on synergies and margin trajectory, but responses indicated satisfaction with current progress.
  • Management maintained an optimistic and confident tone throughout both prepared remarks and Q&A. Brown repeatedly emphasized global expansion and organic growth prospects, while Policarpo addressed cost normalization and synergy realization in detail.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst sentiment was slightly more upbeat, reflecting improved flows and integration updates, while management’s confidence remained consistently high, as evidenced by phrases such as "very optimistic about the opportunity to expand business around the globe."

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • In the current quarter, the company recorded a substantial increase in total client assets, sales flows, and revenue, driven by the Amundi acquisition. The share repurchase plan was expanded from $200 million to $500 million, compared to the previous quarter where the plan remained at $200 million.
  • Integration of Amundi is further along, with $70 million in net expense synergies achieved by quarter end, compared to $50 million at closing in the prior quarter.
  • Management continued to highlight margin guidance of 49%, as in the previous quarter, but actual adjusted EBITDA margin was above that target both quarters.
  • Analyst focus shifted from initial integration and expense synergy questions last quarter to more granular questions on expense wind-down timing, margin drivers, and international strategy this quarter.
  • Management maintained strong confidence in organic and global growth, reiterating strategic priorities around international expansion and product launches.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management referenced $53 million in acquisition-related restructuring and integration costs as a significant onetime item for the quarter, with expectations for these expenses to decline in subsequent quarters.
  • There is ongoing integration risk with the Amundi acquisition, though $70 million of the targeted $110 million in synergies has already been achieved.
  • Analyst questions flagged the pace of expense normalization and the need for continued execution on international distribution and fixed income product growth.

Final Takeaway

Victory Capital’s second quarter marked a transformative period, with record-high client assets, robust sales momentum, and the successful integration of the Amundi U.S. business. Management reported rapid progress on synergy realization, expanded the share buyback plan to $500 million, and continued to invest in global distribution and product diversification. The company remains focused on margin discipline and organic growth, positioning itself to capitalize on global opportunities across both active and passive investment strategies.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Kratos stock jumps after Q2 earnings, revenue beat estimates
data.description: Shares of Kratos Defense & Security Solutions (NASDAQ:KTOS) jumped as much as 11% on Friday morning, a day after the aerospace and defense company reported second-quarter revenue of $351.5 million, up 17.1% year over year and well ahead of Wall Street’s consensus estimate of $305.8 million. Adjusted earnings per share came in at $0.11, also beating analyst expectations of $0.09.
